"The Philosopher's Stone is produced by means of the greening and growing nature. Hali the philosopher says thereof:  'This Stone rises in growing, greening things.'  Wherefore when the green is reduced to its former nature, whereby things sprout and come forth in ordained time, it must be decocted and putrefied in the way of our secret art." 
 
The image is the second illustration from Splendor Solis, the most gorgeously illustrated of all the old alchemical texts. The text on the ribbon that rises from the alchemical vessel says "Let us go in search of the natures of the four elements."

comments.title: Texts on Transits
comments.content:
A few weeks ago at my enquiry you mentioned that there exists an embarrassing dearth of recent astrological texts on transits. You remarked that you knew of none more recent than the one you happened to be reading at the time, dating from 1942 if I recall correctly.

Would you mind mentioning what that book is titled, and who is the author? I would like to have a shot at finding it. Also if there are any other such books from days of yore I would like to find out about them as well.

To your knowledge, do any of the classic astrological or occult authors, such as Agrippa, Ficino or Wm. Lilly treat the topic at any length? If so, can you say in what books or chapters?

If I were to study astrology seriously, I think this is where I would probably begin. Having first thereby helped myself, I imagine it would put me in a position to be of use to others through the same knowledge.

replies.title: Re: Texts on Transits
replies.content:
It's not transits in general -- there are books on big-picture transits, such as those of the slow outer planets -- but daily transits across the natal chart that haven't gotten anything like the attention they deserve. The book I know of that covers this is The Daily Use of the Ephemeris by Elizabeth Aldrich, it was published by David McKay in 1942. I have no idea how readily available it might be.

My book on the subject is in outline and I already have a publisher lined up for it. I'll post something when it's available for advance ordering.

replies.title: Re: Texts on Transits
replies.content:
I'm glad to hear you're working on a book about transits! In a lifetime of studying my personal horoscope, I have found clarity about the effects of significant transits only in retrospect.

Today I went looking for possible hints about classical sources on transits (Lilly, Ficino, etc.) and didn't find much. I did find this interesting comment thread on a Skyscript forum, which offers some possible explanations for the dearth of sources.

http://skyscript.co.uk/forums/viewtopic.php?t=7000&sid=bc411b140dccc92ab7b7f86ef02ba2d3

One commenter writes:

"You will see when you'll start to read the old authors that the transits in most of the cases were last on the list of the hierarchy of prediction. Not that they are not important, but in order for their effect to be influential in significant measure, those planets need to be activated through some other technique of time rulership or to traverse some activated place in the chart."

and another:

"While a modern astrologer might look at transits as determinants of new events, a traditional astrologer usually looks at transits as manifestations (not determinants) of events that are promised in the natal chart AND constrained within the times when the planet(s) is activated via one of the timelord procedures..."

There are some case studies in classical astrology of predictions that turned out to be uncannily accurate, and may offer some insights into working with transits. For example, the prediction of the London fire of 1666 by William Lilly, and the prediction of French King Henry II's death in 1559 by Gauricus, are two fascinating stories.

https://www.skyscript.co.uk/fire.html

https://www.skyscript.co.uk/gauricus.html

replies.title: Re: Texts on Transits
replies.content: Interesting. I certainly agree that a transit won't bring anything that contradicts the natal chart, but it still works very well, say, to submit a manuscript to a new publisher when the Moon is applying to a trine with one's natal Mercury!

replies.title: Re: Texts on Transits
replies.content: I have a copy of Robert Hand's Planets in Transit (1976). It covers transits of all the planets, so, for example, for Sun conjunct Venus, it states, "The central issue today is [etc.]"

replies.title: Re: Texts on Transits
replies.content: Thank you -- that's worth knowing.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
The Llewellyn edition of Agrippa's Three Books simply reprints the text of the only English translation ever made, which was published way back in 1651. Tyson (the editor) added paragraphing to the chapters, modernized the spelling, and added a huge number of explanatory notes. Yes, the 1651 translation is just so-so. But it's all anyone has in English.

If you can read Agrippa's original Latin, there are two good editions available, by Karl Anton Nowotny (Graz, Austria, 1967) and by V. Perrone Campagni (Leiden, 1992). The first is almost unobtainable now and was always very very expensive, and the second is simply very expensive--as opposed to very very expensive. Also, you can find Agrippa's own 1533 printing of his Latin text online for free downloading.

replies.title: Re: Orphic hymns and planetary works
replies.content:
>>Has it worked well for you?

I'm rather curious about this, so I'll share a data point in the hopes of hearing some others.

I recite two of the Orphic hymns that correspond to the planets daily, but it's specifically as part of a devotional practice to the Greek gods in question. I use Thomas Taylor's translation with the original Greek personal names rather than the Roman versions.

As far as I can tell, it's worked well for me, though as it's a devotional practice, I'm not expecting specific "results" per se. Still, I've noticed benefits but haven't noticed drawbacks in around a year and a half of daily practice with the Orphic Hymns, which I started after around five years of prior devotional practice that was less consistent and mostly used ex tempore prayers.

From this experience, I do not believe that the Greek gods are the same as the planetary forces. Sharing some (but not all) correspondences and being therefore highly capable in mediating the planetary forces if that was requested of them perhaps, but not being identical.

Notably, the benefits appear to be consistent with a relationship with a person--guidance through oracles and dreams, regular divine presence, and occasional mystical experiences. All of this constellates around the gods' associations as per historical Greek sources, but does not precisely fit the planetary associations. It also seems to me to have the feeling of being guided by a person rather than the added force to all life areas relating to the astrological associations that I would expect from astrological magic, but I have never used astrological magic, so I am only speculating from what others have said on that last part. I do however study astrology and can say that one of the corresponding planets is dignified by rulership and exaltation but combust in a cadent house and making no other aspects, and the other is peregrine in a succedent house with no aspects. I don't recall if you (JMG) consider those debilitated enough to not use astrological magic with.

Following my early results (which I think included divination, but it was a while ago and I no longer have the records) I only use Greek names and do not treat the Greek and Latin names as equivalents, only using the Latin names when I'm talking about astrology or Roman Polytheism. I also began the devotional practice some time before beginning to study astrology.

comments.title: Pathworking questions

replies.title: Re: Pathworking questions

1) That was unclear, wasn't it? No, you don't do a separate pathworking for the spheres. That's not a pathworking, anyway -- it's called a sphere working, and it's a different thing. You can follow the instructions in Paths of Wisdom exactly, doing one Path at a time.

2) See above. When formulating the sphere you start from, simply imagine it around you.

3) Depends on how much information you get. I did around 5 pathworkings per path, with a solid week of meditation on the material from each one.

4) Thank you!

replies.title: Re: Magic and state
replies.content:
No, it won't get an eye roll. Psychic fork bending was a common thing back in the 1970s, and it's really quite odd to watch. What happens is that the person doing it holds the fork in one hand and uses one thumb to rub the "neck," the place where the handle joins the head, which is usually the thinnest part. When the general did it, he concentrated intently on the fork and then belted out "BEND!" in a parade-ground voice -- and it bent. It looked as though the metal of the neck had suddenly gone limp, and then just as suddenly hardened again, with the head dangling at a sharp angle. I'm perfectly willing to admit that it might have been some kind of trick, but that's not what it looked like.

It's not magic as I know it -- that is to say, it's not causing change in consciousness in accordance with will. If it's not simply clever fakery, it's something very remarkable.

comments.title: National myths: Britain
comments.content:
Under your post about Johnny Appleseed, there has been some discussion about national archetypes for Britain and for Germany.

A few weeks ago you said that magical groups which took Arthurian legend as their foundation myth generally ended in failure. Is this specific to magical groups, or are there other problems with Arthuriana as a myth to live by?

Is the association with failure because Arthur is said to have died in battle, rather than peacefully as a victorious king.

National empires rise and fall, it is the way of them, and so if he is a British national myth he doesn't seem to have been bad for the country. Many other countries have not had such periods of success. (Though maybe that also means further to fall.)

Then on the subject of Robin Hood. If there is success in the Robin Hood myth, that would, I assume, be in the versions where he is a dispossessed nobleman who regains his old lands. But that was a later addition to a medieval myth, and there are also numerous 'debunking' versions in modern fantasy fiction in which he isn't a dispossessed nobleman either. Depending on which version you choose, Robin Hood seems like a myth with the greatest affinity for protestors and those who are content to stay in the counterculture, or for the downwardly mobile who want to make their way up again. Or is that too limited a view of the myth?

A whole blog post on some of these popular myths might be interesting.

replies.title: Re: National myths: Britain
replies.content:
The association with failure in the Arthurian legend isn't just a matter of the fact that he died in battle. Everything he tried to accomplish ended up tumbling to the ground -- and of course that's true historically as well as in legend; Roman Britain, of which he was the last effective defender, imploded messily starting half a century or so after his death, which is why people speak English in most of Britain today. Read any good account of Arthur's reign and the long bitter descent from the glory of its beginning to the utter collapse of its end is a central element. That's part of what makes it so enduring and powerful a theme for writers, poets, and artists -- but it makes it exceedingly tricky to work with in any magical context. I know of any number of groups that set out to do magical work on an Arthurian basis, and all of them followed the same trajectory to failure; yes, there was usually at least one person acting out the role of Mordred, too.

Robin Hood, by contrast, is the founding myth of a counterculture. It's very effective in that role; Robin's triumph wasn't that he regained his lands (as in the later romantic versions), but that he and his fellow outlaws created a splendid, successful alternative society in defiance of everything the authorities could throw at them. If English mages set out to put energy into the idea of "Robin Hood's England," an invisible country of individual freedom in defiance of the establishment, that could be an extremely successful move. (Of course the other nations on the same island each have their own archetypes and energies, and would be wiser to pursue those.)

replies.title: Re: National myths: Britain
replies.content:
Thank you very much for this interesting and detailed reply.

You're right, I should have said England rather than Britain. Apologies to any Scottish and Welsh readers!

Which books on Arthur would you recommend?

As regards Robin Hood, I see what you mean about the creation of a counterculture, and that makes it exciting in a way. However, isn't harassment of the counterculture by the authorities (the Sheriff) a problem implicated in the myth?

Whereas you guys who have Johnny Appleseed, he was mostly loved and celebrated for being who he was, and didn't have the amount of legal trouble that you might consider Robin Hood had. However, we have what we have as national myth and most of us aren't in a position to create new ones out of whole cloth.

There is also the more general notion of the English eccentric, though when reading about the habits of some historical examples, it's easy to think that few people would get away with the same these days. Perhaps we need to be more positive about them?

replies.title: Re: National myths: Britain
replies.content: If you're going to do anything with Arthur, start with Geoffrey of Monmouth, the medieval writer who popularized the story, and then go on to Sir Thomas Malory, whose edition is the classic English version. With Robin Hood, yes, that's one of the things you get with the positive dimension -- but remember that the Sheriff always ended up looking like an idiot. As for the grand tradition of English eccentrics, for heavens's sake, yes!  Affirm them, glory in them, wallow in the fact that England has more astonishingly colorful figures per square mile than any other part of the world. (Well, other than Wales, which can match you one for one.)

It's much more complex. No human being, other than a very advanced adept or saint, can know what another person's karma is. If somebody does something nasty to you, for example, it's entirely possible that the action is payback for something nasty you did to them in another life, and if you take revenge you'll just guarantee that you get whacked even harder later on. That's why the best response to nasty behavior on the part of other people is usually just to walk away -- drop them from your life, by whatever means are necessary, but let karma take care of the rest of it.

If you choose to take revenge, that act sets karmic patterns in motion that you'll have to face up to later on, and you have no way of knowing what those patterns might be -- again, unless you're an advanced adept or saint. If duty or some other factor requires you to take vengeance, accept those consequences with a whole heart as part of the challenge life throws at you, and try not to complain when they hit.

Hi JMG,

In honor of our current historical moment, I've been asked to write a popular piece on plague magic, past and present. I'll be pointing out the obvious: occult sciences and technologies tend to boom during pandemics, at least during the last millennium. Here the Black Death is of course Exhibit A -- as far as I can tell it, together with the Mongol conquest that made it possible, is directly and indirectly responsible for what I've taken to calling the Occult-Scientific Revolution in the Islamic heartlands during the 14th to 17th centuries, paralleled by the European Renaissance boom in occultism, likewise precipitated by mass die-off. So I'll briefly walk readers through that fun episode, as well a few later examples of plague magic from the Ottoman Empire and Qajar Iran. (Suggestively enough, it appears the same magic-square talismans were used to counter plague/cholera and colonialism simultaneously throughout the 19th-century Islamic world. After all, Jung himself described the colonialist project as a form of demon possession!) But I'm not familiar with either late antique or modern examples, which would be useful for fleshing out my argument for a popular audience.

So I have three quick questions for you on this theme, if I may:

1) Do you also have the sense that there's a close correlation between pandemics and the popularity of occult science historically?

2) Do you think it's legitimate to extend the principle to include mass extinction threats more generally, such as the specter of nuclear annihilation during the Cold War, which actually turned out to be great for occult technologies like remote viewing, or WWI, with Dion Fortune and co. waging magic battles? That is, are plague and war equally good for magic?

3) What are your favorite instances of plague magic, from antiquity to the present, Latinate or otherwise?

I had a chance to look through Thorndike more carefully today. Curiously enough, his treatment of the 14th century (in sharp contrast to his treatment of earlier and later centuries) has almost nothing to say about the theory or the practice of magic at that time. That volume, unlike many of his other volumes in the series, is almost wholly devoted to the proto-scientific study of astronomy/astrology, alchemy and medicine during that century. He does mention the Black Death and similar plagues fairly often, but only in a medical context.

On another, somewhat related note, the famous anthropologist Bronisław Malinowski notes, in his treatments of magic and its uses among the Trobriand Islanders in the Pacific Ocean, that in general they use magic when the risks of an activity are great and/or the stakes are high, but not for other activities of theirs--for example, when fishing out on the open ocean versus fishing in sheltered coral lagoons. I don't remember precisely where in his books he notes this, but it was somewhere in his "Argonauts of the Western Pacific," his "Coral Gardens and Their Magic," and/or his book of shorter essays "Magic, Science and Religion." He makes quite a point of the contrast, which--obviously--supports the point you are making.

This isn't commonly noticed, but: Zoe Quinn, the central figure of the Gamergate controversy - the internet-spanning conflict which prefigured, and possibly shaped the cultural battle lines and journalists' attitudes leading into, the 2016 Brexit/Trump rebellions against the mainstream's moral authority to demonize people by association for siding against its abuse of its moral authority to demonize people by association - at some point before or possibly even at the time that the controversy got started (August 16, 2014), on one of the two forums where it got started (Something Awful), was literally using the name "Eris" as her forum handle.

A competing take on the relation between rationalism, postmodernism, and nihilism is given by David Chapman's article expressing concern about the future of civilization (https://meaningness.com/metablog/stem-fluidity-bridge). Chapman sees rationalism as a vital stepping stone, the original postmodernism theorists' works as a viable next step, and nihilistic dysfunction as a side effect of an intergenerational breakdown of postmodernism academia's vision, caused by teaching postmodernism to students too early in their intellectual development.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
Hi JMG,

Alternative history books often focus on Templars that emigrated to Scotland after the great purge in France. But what about the Templars in other countries like Portugal, Cyprus, Italy and elsewhere? Did they also go underground and keep their tradition alive?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
The alternative history books have apparently managed not to notice that there were already plenty of Templars in Scotland, as in most other European countries. The claims for an emigration there are shaky at best, as research into the surviving French Templars don't show any such mass movement. Some seem to have gone to ground in southern France, whence some later Templar orders trace their heritage. Others in the various little kingdoms that later became Germany did the same, and other Templar orders come from that source. The Order in Scotland had substantial properties and a good many knights and other members, and it was those who assisted Robert the Bruce at Bannockburn and elsewhere, and ultimately fed into the Masonic end of Templary.

In Portugal, it's documented that the Templars renamed themselves the Knights of Christ and, under the patronage of the King of Portugal, remained active until 1789. In Aragon, similarly, the Templars became the Order of Montesa. In other countries, the order was dissolved and its members either joined non-military monastic orders or entered secular life. How many of them passed on anything to anyone else is a complicated question that awaits a lot of further research.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Is it significant? Yes. The conjunction of Jupiter and Saturn happens every 20 years and marks a significant shift in eras. Because of the way their orbits mesh, those conjunctions happen in signs of the same element for extended periods. When the conjunctions shift from one element to another, that's a Grand Mutation, and it marks the end of one era of world history and the beginning of another.

That's what's happening this December 21. Jupiter and Saturn have had their conjunctions in Earth signs since 1842, the last Grand Mutation, and now they're shifting into Air signs, where they will remain until 2219. A set of historical conditions that have been in place since Queen Victoria was young woman is going away, and a new set that hasn't been active for almost 600 years is coming into play.

As for how to celebrate it, however you want to; you could simply make an acknowledgment of the change part of your normal solstice celebration.

replies.title: Tech work
replies.content:
There are many different roles in (what I assume you mean) "computer work". Just as a mechanic is very different than a truck driver and only in the general sense they both do "car work".

1. Computer programmers are akin to wizards. They work with symbols in order to unite mental level entities (design patterns, algorithms) with physical realities (actual applications that make the computer do useful work). In this line of work I find that is paramount to be able to jump up and down in a tower of abstractions. Turtles all the way down and all that.

2. System administrators are more like priesthood. They deal with the same realities that programmers do, and sometimes benefit from knowing how to code, but their focus is elsewhere. The tools of their trade are a number of rituals that ensure and ongoing and harmonious relationship with the idols we have build for ourselves.

3. Testers/QA analysts: if we have mages and we have mystics, it's not too far a stretch to call testers the occultists of informatics. While they share the same basic theory and benefit from some practical (basic) training in the other two categories, what makes them thick is their capacity of observation&analysis. Theirs is the hunt of the edge cases, the disharmonies in the system. They can fix it if its a simple enough case, or report it to the programmers if the scope is wide and the effect of potential fixes hard to predict.

4. Technical support: they represent the community side of clergy work. They need to have good communication skills to understand what customers are struggling with. They must educate the novice user, but also understand that we are ultimately humans and the system should ideally fit our thought processes, not the other way around.

replies.title: Re: "Generation Less"
replies.content: If I may - Australia and New Zealand are both places where the environment is not particularly friendly to human hubris. New Zealand is also quite connected to its Maori heritage - Mount Taranaki now has personhood, and a lot of the Maori families have land rights that pre-date European arrival.

replies.title: Re: "Generation Less"
replies.content:
It is a curious effect of NZ being resource poor (particularly fossil fuels), and too small and isolated to work around that.

The country has been coasting on the back of a vast post-WWII hydro investment when the country was one-of the wealthiest (per-capita) in the world on the back of exports to war ravaged UK and Europe, and could thus afford to import resources for large projects.

But after exports sharply dropped in the 70s (as Europe recovered, and the UK joined the EEC), combined with the oil shock, the economy was kneecapped and never really recovered. There have been no significant energy infrastructure investments since (the last projects were finally completed with much difficulty in the 80s) and no fossil fuels to underwrite an expanding energy demand.

As a result energy constraints and issues have been a limiting factor on the economy for a long time - the EECA [1] who are driving this campaign was created as a response to that pressure. They have now been doing work like this for almost 30 years, lately under the guise of helping with 'climate change'; but the country doesn't have much choice, and the drivers are really economic.

[1] https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Energy_Efficiency_and_Conservation_Authority)

comments.title: accidentally summoning angels?
comments.content:
About a year ago I was with my husband driving in a snowstorm at night and the roads were very icy. I was absolutely terrified that we were going to slide off the road, as we kept mildly losing control of the vehicle. Under a lot of personal anxiety, I reached out in silent prayer for some kind of divine help, and two very weird beings came into my mind's eye.

Two floating faces without a body, surrounded by very detailed ornamental oval cartouches that framed their faces almost like a lion's mane. They were virtually identical to one another, but one face was floating in a slightly blue energy field and the other was in a red energy field.

They were both strikingly ugly, with hooked noses, they look like carved statues of humans with a touch of gargoyle thrown in. And when they came into my awareness, I also heard a low, consistent, monotonous guttural throat sound that was more like a vibratory noise than a voice. They've never spoken to me but seemed to understand my intention of wanting some help with the car trip, as I then felt as if they were traveling alongside the car on either side. I started to calm down after this interaction, though at first it was a little bizarre and disarming, but we got where we needed to go safely and upon reflection, I felt that these beings meant us no harm.

Ever since then, I have found I have been able to contact them with relative ease. I ask them to go with my husband when he goes on trips, which is quite often, for work. They don't seem to ever dialogue or converse, except I can tell they definitely understand direct intention, so I kind of just "will" them into my consciousness and they show up and they have always helped me with specifically sending them off with my husband to be sure he travels safely (which he has). From what I can personally tell, they have almost no real "personality" but seem pretty neutral or slightly positive and neutral at the same time. In spite of their stark ugliness and the strangeness of having no idea what they are, I felt intuitively since that night that it was safe to work with them for protection around trips and traveling.

Then, last week I was in a shop with a friend and came across some cards that had depictions of 72 angels of the Kabbalah. I was genuinely shaking when I came across the illustration for an angel titled "Mikhael" because the strikingly ugly angel illustrated on the card looked eerily similar to the two beings I've been receiving help from for the last year, and one of the three strengths attributed to this angel (by the author of the deck) is "giving safe travel."

I have no background in Kabbalah, nor have ever had any interest in angels. But this experience has me quite shook.

I'm wondering if you think it's possible that in a moment of desperation, I summoned a foreign-to-me angelic form of energy that I had no conscious knowledge of, but which very much exists outside of me?

If anyone else wants to add anything I'd love to hear...I have often wondered if I made them up since that first night, with how just plain weird they are, I figured it had to be a figment of my imagination or maybe something that just made me feel a bit better and safer...but seeing the mirror image depiction (and who thinks of angels as being so ugly? I never have seen that!) and the description of that angel's function lining up so clearly with what I experienced, makes me feel like it's not a personal manifestation of my imagination but perhaps a connection springing from some deeper source.

replies.title: Re: accidentally summoning angels?

That very likely is what happened. You may have had a connection with those angels from a previous incarnation. As for angels being ugly, the bizarre notion that angels look like debutantes with oversized pigeon wings has no basis in scripture or tradition. Here's a description of four angels as recorded by a witness:

"Out of the midst thereof came the likeness of four living creatures. And this was their appearance; they had the likeness of a man. And every one had four faces, and every one had four wings. And their feet were straight feet; and the sole of their feet was like the sole of a calf's foot: and they sparkled like the colour of burnished brass. And they had the hands of a man under their wings on their four sides; and they four had their faces and their wings. Their wings were joined one to another; they turned not when they went; they went every one straight forward. As for the likeness of their faces, they four had the face of a man, and the face of a lion, on the right side: and they four had the face of an ox on the left side; they four also had the face of an eagle...As for the likeness of the living creatures, their appearance was like burning coals of fire, and like the appearance of lamps: it went up and down among the living creatures; and the fire was bright, and out of the fire went forth lightning."

Angels are terrifying. I suspect the ones that appeared to you went out of their way to tone down their appearance so they didn't scare the bejesus out of you. C.S. Lewis, who knew a great deal about angels, put a similar bit into the final scene of his novel Perelandra.

comments.title: Mysticism and occultism
comments.content: Sorry if this is an utter novice question, but I just wanted to get a proper definition on what the differences are between mysticism and occultism? I've seen both mentioned on here as different paths, but don't actually know how they differ.

replies.title: Re: Mysticism and occultism
replies.content: As with most words, these two get used in different ways by different people. The idea in Western occult tradition is that there are two currents flowing through the cosmos, one descending from the divine (however defined) into creative manifestation, the other rising up from manifestation back to the divine. Mystics work with the rising current and seek to leave manifest existence behind and become one with the divine. Occultists work with the descending current and seek to serve the divine as instruments of creation in the world of manifestation. Both are valid paths, but different people are called to one or the other.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content: JMG, I know that you've fielded this question before, but do you have any general advice on getting started on a writing career, in terms of finding a niche, making money from it, and motivating oneself to actually get started? How and where can one start from scratch later in life, with no previous experience? Any books or other resources that helped you would be greatly appreciated.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
1) Have a day job. Unless you're insanely lucky, it'll take ten years of hard work before you start making any significant amount of money, and more than that before you can support yourself.

2) Start writing. Make time to write every single day. Treat it as you would any other serious pursuit, and make it a priority. As for how to motivate yourself, only you can do that, and if you aren't able to do so, you might be better off doing something else. There are no gimmicks; either you choose to become a writer, or you don't.

3) Read good books in the subject areas and genres in which you want to write. Now and again stop and examine a sentence or a paragraph or a page closely, to figure out how the author is doing whatever he or she is doing. That will teach you more than anything else.

4) Stay away from writing groups, writing classes, etc. I've seen more potential talents ruined by such things than by any other source. Writing takes place between you and your keyboard.

5) Consider writing nonfiction first to break into print. 80% of all would-be writers want to write fiction. 80% of the books published each year are nonfiction. So you can be one of the 80% of people chasing the 20% of options, or you can be one of the 20% of people chasing the 80% of options, take your pick.

6) Start off selling to small publishers and fringe venues. It's much easier to get into print that way, and once you're in print you can build from there. Going for the big boys right away pretty much guarantees rejection.

7) Always be working on something new. When you send one story off to a prospective magazine or what have you, have another one already started. When you get your first book manuscript on its way to a publisher, start writing a different book that same day.

Those are the rules that have worked for me. Of course your mileage may vary!

comments.title: Geomantic Triplicities question
comments.content:
Hello JMG, thanks as always for sharing your knowledge and wisdom!

I have a question regarding your descriptions of the Geomantic Triplicities. Previously I had asked if you remembered your original source for the descriptions of the triplicities, and you said that you no longer remember other than that it was definitely not Robert Fludd's Fasciculus Geomanticus, which features different descriptions for the triplicities. (If something happens to have jogged your memory in the intervening time, I would still love to know.)

Last week I happened to take a look at your descriptions of the Triplicities in the material included in The Celtic Golden Dawn, and I was surprised to discover that it is not the same as what had been featured in The Art and Practice of Geomancy. At first it seemed simply like an expansion of the triplicity guidelines, breaking down the information in each triplicity into into specific information that can be sought in individual Mothers, Daughters, and Nieces, in a way that could be inferred in a straightforward fashion by reading the triplicites as mini-courts. But then I noticed that when it came to the 3rd and 4th triplicities, what was shared in the CGD wasn't merely an expansion of the information from APG; some assignments had shifted places between the 3rd and 4th triplicities.

So my question, if you don't mind my asking, is: what is the source for the breakdown of triplicities into individual houses which you provide in the CGD? A different classical source, the product of your own meditations/revelations, something else? Are the differences from APG's presentation of triplicities something from that came from a choice on your part (and/or the spirits of the CGD) to uniquely differentiate CGD's way of geomancy, or are the differences simply a product of using slightly differing reference materials?

replies.title: Re: Geomantic Triplicities question
replies.content: The differences come from years of further experience with geomantic charts. The CGD triplicities fit my experience and that of my students more closely than the more traditional set.

replies.title: Re: Geomantic Triplicities question

comments.title: Randonautica: app, game, occult practice?
comments.content:
There's an app called Randonautica, which generates random coordinates in your area using a random numbers from a quantum random number generator (at a university in Australia). The intended use is to attempt to visit the random site, and use the experience of the place or the attempt to reach it to address a prior intention such as finding an idea to solve a problem or answering a question. Reporting on the results is also emphasized. Here's a more detailed description: Randonautica Explained

To me this sounds very much like an occult practice, and one that could have real value. Elements of divination, discursive thought, and journaling would be involved in perceiving associations between the visited place (or the attempt) and the intention and then describing them in a report. There's no banishing involved, but the use of material surroundings to engage the imagination would seem to put fairly safe bounds on the range of imagination involved. I'm a bit house-bound right now as my wife is recovering from a medical procedure, but as it's not all that dissimilar from my accustomed ramblings, I plan to try it out when I can.

I'm not sure the "quantum" trappings are necessary; one could presumably drop a planchette onto a map instead, or use some other randomizing method.

Users frequently report coincidences in the process. Some of them have been quite dramatic or frightening, such as finding a dead body or being "randomly" directed to a relative's grave site. However, it's difficult to assess how truthful such reports have been.

Do you have any thoughts about this? Is my assessment reasonable? Apart from the obvious potential physical hazards of visiting unfamiliar places, do you see any risk in it?

replies.title: Re: Randonautica: app, game, occult practice?
replies.content: Interesting. It sounds like it could be fun, and it would make a very good adjunct method of will training. Here's a location -- now get to it!

comments.title: Changing the past
comments.content:
Archdruid,

How might I go about changing the past?

Gratitude.

replies.title: Re: Changing the past
replies.content:
You can't. All you can do is change your attitude to it. Magic, remember, is the art and science of causing change in consciousness according to will; the past affects you through your judgments and attitudes concerning it, and those can be transformed. The events themselves? Omar Khayyam had something to say about that:

"The moving finger writes; and, having writ,
Moves on: nor all thy piety nor wit
Shall lure it back to cancel half a line,
Nor all thy tears wash out a word of it."

replies.title: Re: Changing the past
replies.content:
From seeing how other supernatural phenomena relate to time, you'd think it would be possible. It sure seems to work when you don't already know whether things went the way you wanted. But intuitively, it doesn't seem like you could really expect that to carry over to the case where you did already know how things had gone, except in the case where you had already been a certain kind of person: the kind who would put in the same effort when you remembered things going the way you wanted as when you remembered things going the way you didn't want. In particular, that would mean that the version of you that remembered things going the way you wanted would have had to independently believe that there had been a realistic possibility of something going wrong, so that they would be willing to put in the effort to retroactively prevent it. (This might also work on an unconscious level: there might be a kind of unconscious luck that depends on having already been thoroughly willing to realistically acknowledge how things might have gone wrong.)

This might imply an aspect of time-magic to the practice of giving thanks for the blessings you do have.

Some Zen commentaries make it sound as though the way some kinds of concentration meditation work is by keeping you distracted, so that the things your mind would normally cling to can be stolen away bit by bit. Maybe that would also help, if the problem is that the version of you for whom things didn't go the way you wanted is attached to having their timeline stay real. But that sounds hazardous: what decides what else might get stolen away?

Really, all of this sounds hazardous (except probably the giving thanks part, though see Luke 18:9-14), and should ideally only be attempted with the advice of an experienced teacher or divine guidance. It doesn't seem like humans naturally think about time in the right way to be able to independently recognize potential blunders in time workings, any more than in money workings or political workings, all of which involve massive tug-of-wars over who will get to make which things real. There is some merit in just learning to live with what would have naturally tended to happen, and that may include learning to live with what your alternate self naturally would have failed to help retroactively prevent. In any case there'd be no avoiding your eventual "true" past, which would just be exactly your part of what's left over from all that tug-of-war and natural happenstance, once everyone including you gives up on affecting how real that past is.
